Ongoing mechanical ventilation into 13–24h suggests slower recovery and higher severity, which is associated with AKI risk. However, mean ventilation status is still an indirect proxy rather than a direct renal insult.

Persistent mechanical ventilation at 25–36h is a stronger marker of ongoing critical illness (shock, pulmonary edema, low cardiac output) that commonly co-tracks with AKI. Still, it is not kidney-specific and acts mainly as a severity proxy.

Any mechanical ventilation in 0–12h is expected after cardiac surgery, limiting specificity for AKI. As an extreme/presence indicator it modestly reflects immediate post-op severity but is conservative in this epoch.
Mechanical ventilation at any point in 13–24h indicates delayed extubation and higher acuity, which correlates with AKI risk. It remains an indirect predictor and overlaps with the mean indicator.

Mechanical ventilation at any time in 25–36h is a meaningful leading indicator of persistent organ dysfunction and hemodynamic stress associated with AKI. Prefer this presence/extreme indicator over the mean to reduce redundancy.

A minimum indicator for mechanical ventilation in 0–12h (i.e., whether it was continuously present) is largely driven by routine post-op ventilation practices. It adds little beyond other ventilation presence features and is not kidney-specific.

Continuous mechanical ventilation throughout 13–24h suggests sustained critical illness and delayed recovery, indirectly increasing AKI risk. It is still a proxy and collinear with other ventilation indicators.

Continuous mechanical ventilation across 25–36h implies persistent respiratory/cardiac failure and ongoing severity close to the creatinine outcome window, which is associated with AKI. It remains indirect but more informative in this late epoch.

Measurement frequency is mainly a documentation/acuity proxy rather than a renal mechanism, and early (0–12h) ventilation is routine after cardiac surgery. Weak, indirect link to AKI via overall illness severity only.
Counts of mechanical oxygen-device entries reflect monitoring intensity more than physiology. Prolonged ventilation may correlate with severity, but as a _measured feature it should remain low.

By 25–36h, ongoing mechanical ventilation suggests persistent cardiopulmonary dysfunction that can reduce renal perfusion, but this is still a behavioral proxy (_measured) rather than a direct AKI driver.
Being on no oxygen device immediately post-op is uncommon and mostly reflects extubation practices rather than kidney injury biology. Early epoch is noisy and transient.

More time on no oxygen device may indicate faster recovery and lower overall severity, indirectly lowering AKI risk. Mean aggregation is less informative than an extreme/indicator of persistent support needs.

Sustained time without oxygen support late (25–36h) suggests clinical stability and less shock/low output, indirectly associated with lower AKI risk. Still not a direct renal perfusion or nephrotoxin marker.
A binary extreme indicating any period of no oxygen device in the immediate post-op window is dominated by routine extubation timing and documentation. Minimal specific linkage to AKI.
Any time off oxygen support during 13–24h can indicate improving trajectory and lower severity, indirectly associated with less AKI. As a coarse binary, it is not a direct predictor.
Any time on room air/no device in 25–36h suggests recovery and less ongoing cardiopulmonary failure, indirectly lowering AKI risk. Binary extreme is less specific than hemodynamics/renal labs.

If coded as the minimum (i.e., whether the patient was ever not on 'none'), it largely reflects immediate post-op ventilation practices and is not kidney-specific. Early epoch limits interpretability.

Persistent absence of 'none' (i.e., always requiring some oxygen device) suggests ongoing respiratory/cardiac dysfunction and higher illness severity, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Still not a direct renal insult marker.

By 25–36h, continued need for oxygen support throughout the window (no time on 'none') can reflect persistent low output/respiratory failure and higher vasopressor/PEEP needs, which are more plausibly linked to renal hypoperfusion. This is still indirect compared with MAP/urine output/creatinine.
Number of recordings of 'none' is a documentation/monitoring artifact and not a physiologic driver of AKI. Early post-op charting frequency is especially variable.
As a _measured feature, it reflects clinician documentation patterns rather than renal pathophysiology. Weak indirect association with acuity at best.
Even late, measurement frequency is not a mechanistic predictor of AKI and is prone to workflow bias. Keep low per the rubric for _measured variables.

Low-flow oxygen use in the immediate 0–12h period is common post-extubation and mainly reflects respiratory support practices. Mean aggregation in this early window provides little specific signal for AKI risk.

Low-flow oxygen use is a nonspecific marker of mild respiratory support and does not directly reflect renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. In the 13–24h epoch it is more reflective of routine postoperative care than impending AKI.

Persistent need for low-flow oxygen at 25–36h can weakly proxy ongoing cardiopulmonary dysfunction that may correlate with renal hypoperfusion. However it remains indirect and nonspecific for AKI compared with hemodynamics, urine output, or renal labs.
Any low-flow oxygen use in the immediate 0–12h period is common after cardiac surgery and is not a specific AKI signal. As a binary extreme it adds little beyond general postoperative status.
Binary presence of low-flow oxygen during 13–24h is still largely routine and not mechanistically tied to kidney injury. It is less informative than measures of shock, vasopressors, or congestion.

Ongoing requirement for low-flow oxygen by 25–36h may indicate residual heart failure/atelectasis and indirectly correlate with venous congestion or low cardiac output affecting kidneys. Still a weak, nonspecific predictor.

A minimum (binary) indicator of low-flow oxygen in 0–12h is not physiologically interpretable as an AKI trigger and is dominated by routine postoperative oxygen administration. It provides minimal incremental prognostic value.

This binary minimum indicator is not a meaningful representation of severity or duration of hypoxemia/respiratory failure. It is unlikely to add AKI-relevant information beyond other cardiopulmonary variables.
As a binary minimum, it does not capture severity (e.g., hypoxemia, high FiO2) and is not directly linked to renal injury mechanisms. Any association with AKI would be indirect and weak.

Measurement frequency reflects documentation/monitoring behavior rather than renal physiology, and oxygen device recording is routine early post-op. It is unlikely to represent specific concern for AKI.
Frequency of recording low-flow oxygen device is primarily workflow-related and not a direct marker of renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. At most it reflects general acuity, so importance is low.

Even in the 25–36h window, documentation frequency of oxygen device is a weak behavioral proxy and not specific to AKI risk. It should be deprioritized versus physiologic extremes or renal-related labs.

High-flow oxygen use suggests more significant respiratory compromise and may correlate with hemodynamic stress, but in 0–12h it is often transient post-op and not specific for AKI. It is an indirect severity marker only.

Continued high-flow oxygen at 13–24h can indicate persistent cardiopulmonary dysfunction that may contribute to renal hypoperfusion or congestion. Still indirect and less predictive than direct hemodynamic/renal measures.

High-flow oxygen requirement close to the outcome window (25–36h) is a stronger marker of ongoing critical illness (e.g., pulmonary edema/low cardiac output) that can precipitate AKI via hypoperfusion and venous congestion. It remains indirect but more informative than low-flow oxygen.

Any high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h may reflect immediate postoperative instability, but this period is confounded by anesthesia/CPB effects and transient pulmonary issues. As a binary extreme it is only a weak-to-moderate indirect AKI risk marker.

Binary presence of high-flow oxygen during 13–24h indicates higher acuity and possible cardiopulmonary failure, which can secondarily increase AKI risk. However it is nonspecific and collinear with more direct shock/ventilation variables.

Need for high-flow oxygen late (25–36h) suggests ongoing respiratory failure and higher overall illness severity, which can correlate with AKI via hypoxemia/inflammation. It is not a direct renal perfusion marker, so importance is limited.
Early (0–12h) oxygen device choice is heavily influenced by immediate post-op/vent weaning practices and transient instability. It has weak specificity for subsequent creatinine rise.

During 13–24h, high-flow use may reflect evolving pulmonary issues but remains an indirect severity marker rather than a kidney-specific driver. The "min" aggregation adds little pathophysiologic signal beyond presence/absence.

Persistent need for high-flow oxygen in the 25–36h window indicates ongoing hypoxemia/respiratory support needs close to the outcome time, which can contribute to renal stress. Still indirect and less informative than hemodynamics/renal labs.
Measurement frequency reflects documentation/monitoring behavior in the immediate post-op period rather than renal physiology. It is unlikely to add specific predictive value for AKI.
How often high-flow status is recorded is a care-process proxy and not a mechanistic AKI predictor. At most it weakly reflects acuity.
More frequent documentation/orders late may indicate clinician concern and higher acuity near the outcome window. Still a behavioral proxy, so capped at low importance.
First-degree AV block is usually benign and common post-op; measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring. It has minimal direct linkage to renal hypoperfusion or AKI.
Ongoing monitoring/notation of first-degree block is not kidney-specific and rarely drives hemodynamic collapse. Thus it provides weak AKI signal.
Even late, first-degree block typically does not meaningfully reduce cardiac output or renal perfusion. Measurement frequency remains a low-value proxy.
Higher-grade AV block can cause bradycardia and low cardiac output, which can reduce renal perfusion, but early post-op conduction issues are often transient. As a "measured" proxy, it is capped low.

Persistent concern for 2nd/3rd-degree block may indicate hemodynamic instability and potential renal hypoperfusion risk. However, this is still measurement frequency rather than the actual rhythm/hemodynamic effect.

Late monitoring for high-grade block suggests ongoing conduction problems that could impair cardiac output and renal perfusion near the outcome window. As a behavioral proxy feature, importance remains limited.
Bundle branch block is common after cardiac surgery and often does not imply major hemodynamic compromise. Measurement frequency in the immediate period is unlikely to predict AKI.
BBB monitoring/documentation is not a direct marker of renal injury and usually has limited impact on perfusion. As a measured feature, it provides weak prognostic information.
Late BBB documentation may reflect ongoing cardiac conduction abnormalities but typically without strong renal perfusion consequences. Measurement frequency remains a low-specificity acuity proxy.
Ionized calcium abnormalities early post-bypass are common (citrate, transfusion, hemodilution) and are not a direct AKI driver. Mean values in the immediate 0–12h window are especially nonspecific.
Persistently abnormal calcium may reflect illness severity, but it is not an established independent predictor of AKI. The mean further dilutes clinically relevant extremes.

Later calcium derangements can correlate with ongoing critical illness and vasopressor/transfusion needs, indirectly relating to renal hypoperfusion risk. Still not a primary AKI mechanism and mean is less informative than minima.

Transient hypercalcemia in the immediate postoperative period is uncommon and typically treatment-related, with weak linkage to AKI. Extreme high values are not a typical trigger for postoperative AKI compared with hypotension/low Ca.
High ionized calcium later is usually iatrogenic (replacement) and not a recognized AKI predictor. Adds limited pathophysiologic signal for creatinine rise.
Hypercalcemia is not a typical leading indicator of post-cardiac surgery AKI, and often reflects supplementation. Redundant/low-yield compared with low calcium extremes.

Hypocalcemia can accompany massive transfusion/citrate load and hemodynamic instability, indirectly associating with renal hypoperfusion. In 0–12h it is often transient and nonspecific, so score is conservative.

Persistent low ionized calcium may reflect ongoing critical illness, inflammation, or continued transfusion/CRRT risk milieu, indirectly linked to AKI. Min is more pathologic than mean/max but still not a direct renal biomarker.

Later hypocalcemia can be a marker of sustained severity (shock, high transfusion burden) and may track with worsening organ perfusion, increasing AKI risk. The minimum captures the clinically relevant insult better than mean/max.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects routine post-cardiac surgery monitoring and protocolized electrolyte checks rather than renal pathophysiology. Early ordering is not specific for impending AKI.
More frequent checks may indicate ongoing instability or replacement needs, but it is a weak behavioral proxy and not specific for AKI. Keep low to avoid over-weighting care intensity.

Increased late measurement frequency can reflect clinician concern about ongoing instability or large electrolyte shifts (e.g., transfusion/CRRT consideration), which may co-occur with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, so capped low.
Magnesium is commonly supplemented after cardiac surgery and early mean levels are heavily treatment-driven. It has weak direct linkage to postoperative AKI compared with perfusion and nephrotoxins.
Mean magnesium in this phase is usually maintained by replacement protocols and is not an established AKI predictor. Any association is likely confounding by illness severity.

Later magnesium abnormalities can reflect renal handling changes or ongoing critical illness, giving an indirect signal for evolving AKI. Mean values remain less specific than extremes or direct renal labs.
Early hypermagnesemia is typically iatrogenic (replacement) and not a typical causal driver of AKI in this setting. In 0–12h it is especially nonspecific and collinear with mean magnesium.
Hypermagnesemia can reflect reduced renal clearance or heavy supplementation, but it is not a primary AKI driver. In the 13–24h window it is an indirect signal and often treatment-related.
Rising/high magnesium closer to hour 60 can indicate impaired renal excretion and evolving kidney dysfunction. Still less specific than creatinine/urine output and may be confounded by replacement.
Early postoperative hypomagnesemia is common after CPB/hemodilution and usually reflects shifts/repletion rather than renal injury. The 0–12h epoch is noisy and transient.
Low magnesium in this phase more often reflects ongoing replacement needs/diuresis than impending AKI. It has weak direct linkage to creatinine rise.

Persistently low magnesium may correlate with diuretic exposure or poor nutritional status and can accompany high-output states, but it is not a direct AKI marker. Any association with creatinine ratio is likely indirect.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects routine post-cardiac surgery electrolyte monitoring rather than AKI physiology. In the immediate 0–12h period it is standard-of-care.
Repeated magnesium checks are common ICU practice and more related to replacement protocols than renal prognosis. As a behavioral proxy it has limited specificity for AKI.
More frequent magnesium monitoring late may reflect clinician concern for instability or renal clearance issues, but it remains an indirect behavioral signal. Score capped per the _measured rule.

Average base excess early after surgery is influenced by CPB, transfusion, and ventilation changes and is often transient. It can relate to perfusion but is less informative than extremes for AKI risk.

Sustained metabolic acidosis can reflect ongoing hypoperfusion and is associated with AKI risk, but the mean blunts clinically important nadirs. In 13–24h it is moderately informative but not definitive.

Persistently negative base excess closer to hour 60 suggests ongoing metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/shock or renal dysfunction, both linked to AKI. Mean is still less specific than the minimum but can capture sustained derangement.

High/positive base excess (metabolic alkalosis) is not a typical trigger for AKI and often reflects bicarbonate administration or ventilation/diuretic effects. Early postoperative values are especially confounded.
A high base excess is generally not a direct marker of renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury. It is commonly iatrogenic and weakly related to creatinine rise.
Metabolic alkalosis late is not a strong mechanistic predictor of AKI compared with acidosis/hypoperfusion markers. Any association is likely confounded by diuretics or bicarbonate therapy.

The minimum base excess captures the worst metabolic acidosis, which can reflect low cardiac output, shock, or high lactate states that predispose to AKI. Score tempered because 0–12h derangements are common and may resolve.

A low nadir base excess in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing hypoperfusion or systemic illness, both established AKI risk factors. The minimum is preferred over mean/max as it captures the acute insult.

A very negative base excess late (25–36h) reflects persistent metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/shock or severe illness, which is strongly associated with renal ischemia and subsequent AKI. The minimum captures the worst insult better than mean values.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects ordering behavior in the immediate post-op period and is not a direct physiologic driver of AKI. Early (0–12h) testing is routine after cardiac surgery.
Counts of base excess measurements are a weak proxy for acuity and are not mechanistically linked to kidney injury. In 13–24h this is still commonly monitored and nonspecific.
More frequent acid-base checks late may indicate clinician concern for ongoing shock/poor perfusion, which can precede AKI, but it remains an indirect behavioral marker rather than physiology.

Ordering frequency for direct bilirubin in the first 12 hours is usually routine or driven by liver/hemolysis evaluation and is not specific for AKI risk. As a measured-count feature it should be low importance.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency is a nonspecific marker of clinician attention to hepatic dysfunction/hemolysis and has only an indirect relationship to AKI. As a behavioral proxy, it should remain low.
Late repeated bilirubin checks may signal evolving cholestasis/low-flow state or hemolysis, which can co-occur with multiorgan dysfunction and AKI, but the count itself is still indirect.

Mean WBC in the immediate post-op window is heavily influenced by surgical stress and steroids and is poorly specific for infection-related AKI. Collinearity with WBC extremes further limits added value.
Persistently elevated average WBC may suggest inflammation/infection that can contribute to AKI, but mean values are less informative than extremes and this epoch is still relatively early.
A sustained elevated WBC closer to outcome can reflect infection/SIRS contributing to AKI risk, but mean is still less discriminative than max/min for acute insults.

A high peak WBC early can reflect stress response or early infection/inflammation, which is only an indirect AKI risk factor in the immediate post-op period. Max is preferred over mean for capturing acute inflammatory surges.
A higher WBC peak during 13–24h may indicate evolving infection/SIRS, which can worsen renal perfusion and AKI risk, but remains nonspecific.

A high WBC peak late (25–36h) is more concerning for true infection/SIRS or ongoing inflammation, which is an established contributor to AKI via sepsis-associated renal dysfunction. Max captures the relevant acute signal better than mean.
Low WBC early is uncommon and often reflects dilutional effects or lab variability rather than a direct AKI mechanism. It is less informative than WBC max for inflammatory risk.
WBC nadir in this period is generally nonspecific and not a recognized driver of postoperative AKI compared with hemodynamics, nephrotoxins, or renal labs.
A low WBC late could reflect severe illness/immunosuppression or marrow suppression and may correlate with higher infection risk, but it is still an indirect and nonspecific predictor of AKI.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects routine post-op monitoring rather than a renal mechanism. Early (0–12h) ordering is common after cardiac surgery and is weakly specific for impending AKI.
Test frequency is a behavioral proxy for acuity and is not a direct driver of creatinine rise. In 13–24h it may reflect evolving concern for infection/inflammation but remains nonspecific for AKI.

More frequent WBC checks late (25–36h) can indicate clinician concern for sepsis/SIRS, which can precipitate AKI via hypotension and inflammation. Still indirect and not a physiologic biomarker, so capped low.

PT prolongation can reflect hepatic dysfunction, dilutional coagulopathy, or massive transfusion, which correlate with illness severity and potential renal hypoperfusion. In the immediate 0–12h post-bypass period it is often transient and less predictive.

Persistently elevated mean PT may indicate ongoing hepatic hypoperfusion/critical illness associated with AKI risk. However, extremes (max) better capture clinically meaningful coagulopathy, so mean is down-weighted for collinearity.

A high PT peak suggests significant coagulopathy from shock, liver hypoperfusion, or major transfusion/bleeding, all associated with renal ischemia risk. Early post-op derangements are common, so scored conservatively.

A late (25–36h) PT maximum indicates ongoing or worsening coagulopathy/organ dysfunction and is a strong marker of systemic hypoperfusion or severe illness that commonly co-occurs with AKI. The extreme value best captures the pathologic insult versus mean/min.

Low/normal PT is not a plausible trigger for AKI and mainly reflects adequate coagulation status. The minimum adds little beyond other PT summaries and is not pathophysiologically linked to creatinine rise.
The minimum PT in this window generally represents normal values and is not informative for renal injury risk. It is also redundant given max/mean PT features.
Ordering frequency reflects monitoring/transfusion workflows after surgery rather than renal physiology. In 0–12h it is routine and weakly specific for AKI.
More PT checks may reflect bleeding/anticoagulation management, but this is an indirect proxy and not a direct AKI predictor. Kept low per measurement-frequency rule.
Late frequent PT monitoring can signal ongoing bleeding, liver dysfunction, or escalating critical illness, which can contribute to AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, so capped at 2.

NRBCs can reflect hypoxemia, systemic stress, or severe inflammation, which correlate with organ dysfunction including AKI. In 0–12h post-op, elevations may be transient from surgery/CPB, so predictive value is limited.
Persistent NRBCs suggest ongoing physiologic stress or hypoxia that can accompany renal hypoperfusion. Mean values are less sensitive than peaks for capturing acute insults, so kept modest.

Sustained NRBC presence late (25–36h) is a marker of ongoing critical illness/hypoxemia and has been associated with worse outcomes and multiorgan dysfunction, including AKI. Mean is still less specific than a max but gains relevance due to temporal proximity.

A peak NRBC early may indicate significant perioperative hypoxia/stress, which can contribute to renal ischemic risk. However, immediate post-op peaks can be nonspecific and transient, so scored conservatively.

Elevated NRBCs reflect physiologic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation and can correlate with overall illness severity that predisposes to AKI, but they are not kidney-specific. In the 13–24h trajectory phase, this is an indirect signal rather than a direct driver.

Persistently high NRBCs closer to the outcome suggest ongoing hypoperfusion/inflammation or marrow stress, which often co-tracks with multiorgan dysfunction and AKI risk. Still indirect and non-specific, but more informative in 25–36h.
A low/normal NRBC minimum in the immediate post-op period is not mechanistically linked to AKI and is largely uninformative. Early post-bypass fluctuations make this especially non-specific.

NRBC minimum does not capture a pathologic insult relevant to AKI; absence of NRBCs is not protective in a way that predicts creatinine rise. This aggregation is also less meaningful than an extreme high value.
NRBC minimum remains a weak, non-pathologic signal and does not represent a renal perfusion insult. Prefer max over min to avoid redundant, low-yield collinear summaries.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects ordering behavior and routine CBC/differential patterns post-cardiac surgery rather than renal physiology. In 0–12h it is especially driven by standard post-op monitoring.
How often NRBCs are measured is a weak proxy for acuity and is not a direct AKI mechanism. Ordering frequency is influenced by institutional lab panels and anemia/bleeding evaluation.

More frequent measurement late may reflect clinician concern for ongoing critical illness (e.g., hypoxia, shock, bleeding) that can accompany AKI risk. Still primarily a behavioral proxy, so capped low.

Stress hyperglycemia and glycemic variability are associated with worse outcomes and may contribute to endothelial dysfunction, but mean glucose early post-op is heavily influenced by transient surgical stress and insulin protocols. Mean is less informative than extremes for acute insult.
Sustained dysglycemia can correlate with severity of illness and inflammation, indirectly increasing AKI risk. However, mean values are smoothed and often protocol-driven, limiting specificity.

Persistently elevated mean glucose closer to hour 60 suggests ongoing stress response/infection or poor metabolic control, which is associated with higher AKI risk. Still indirect and less specific than extreme hyper/hypoglycemia.

Extreme hyperglycemia can reflect catecholamine surge and severe stress and is associated with adverse outcomes, but in 0–12h it is commonly transient and treatment-driven. Scored conservatively due to early epoch and collinearity with mean.

Higher glucose peaks during 13–24h may indicate ongoing critical illness, infection, or high-dose vasopressors/steroids, which correlate with AKI risk. Max better captures pathologic excursions than mean in this window.

Late hyperglycemic peaks can be a leading indicator of persistent stress/inflammation and are associated with worse renal outcomes after cardiac surgery. Max captures acute excursions that may track with hemodynamic instability more than mean.

Hypoglycemia can occur with insulin therapy and is associated with increased mortality and physiologic stress, but early post-op lows are often iatrogenic and transient. Min captures the relevant extreme but is down-weighted for the 0–12h epoch.

Hypoglycemia in 13–24h may reflect over-insulinization, hepatic dysfunction, or severe illness and is associated with adverse outcomes that can include AKI. Minimum is the most relevant aggregation for this risk compared with mean.

Hypoglycemia can reflect critical illness, hepatic dysfunction, or excessive insulin and is associated with worse outcomes, but it is not a direct AKI driver. As an extreme in the closest window it may proxy severity rather than renal-specific injury.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects ICU protocol intensity and clinician concern rather than a physiologic mechanism for AKI. In the immediate post-op period glucose checks are routine after cardiac surgery.
Ordering frequency is a behavioral proxy and is commonly protocol-driven in post-cardiac surgery care. It has weak, non-specific linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.

More frequent glucose checks in this later window may indicate ongoing instability (vasopressors, insulin infusion, sepsis), indirectly correlating with AKI risk. Still, it is not a renal biomarker and remains non-specific.

Early thrombocytopenia after CPB is common and often transient, so the mean in 0–12h is a weak discriminator for AKI. Platelets relate indirectly via inflammation/microthrombosis but are not kidney-specific.

Persistently lower platelets can reflect ongoing inflammation, bleeding/consumption, or sepsis, which can accompany AKI, but the mean blunts clinically important nadirs. Moderate indirect relevance in this mid window.

In the leading-indicator window, sustained thrombocytopenia can mark ongoing systemic illness (sepsis, DIC, low-flow state) associated with AKI. Mean is less informative than the nadir, so importance is capped to limit collinearity with platelet_min.
A higher platelet maximum early post-op is not a recognized trigger or marker of AKI. It is largely non-informative compared with thrombocytopenia extremes.
Platelet maxima do not capture the consumptive/inflammatory processes most linked to AKI risk. Limited physiologic plausibility for predicting creatinine rise.
Even close to the outcome, platelet maximum is not a meaningful indicator of renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury. Nadir/low platelets are the clinically relevant direction.

A low platelet nadir immediately after CPB is common and may reflect hemodilution/consumption, with only weak specificity for AKI in this early epoch. It can still proxy severity of bypass-related inflammation.

A low platelet nadir in 13–24h suggests persistent consumption/inflammation, bleeding, or evolving sepsis, which are established contexts for AKI. The minimum better captures pathology than the mean/max.

Thrombocytopenia nadir in the 25–36h window can indicate ongoing systemic inflammatory response, sepsis/DIC, or severe illness with microvascular dysfunction, all strongly associated with subsequent AKI. As an extreme close to hour 60, it is a stronger leading indicator than mean/max.
Platelet measurement frequency is largely routine post-op monitoring and reflects practice patterns more than renal pathophysiology. Weak and non-specific for AKI prediction.
More frequent CBCs may reflect acuity (bleeding/infection workup) but is still a behavioral proxy with limited renal specificity. Score kept low per measurement-frequency rule.
In the later window, increased platelet checks may indicate concern for bleeding, HIT, or sepsis—conditions that can co-occur with AKI. Still indirect and non-mechanistic, so capped at 2.

Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) is not a direct driver or marker of renal perfusion/AKI; early postoperative shifts are often dilutional/transfusion-related. In the 0–12h window it adds little specific prognostic value for creatinine rise.
MCH has minimal mechanistic linkage to AKI risk compared with hemodynamics, nephrotoxins, and renal labs. The 13–24h average is unlikely to capture a kidney-relevant insult.

Even close to the outcome window, MCH is not an established predictor of postoperative AKI. Any association would be indirect (anemia/bleeding/transfusion) and better captured by hemoglobin/hematocrit rather than MCH.
An elevated MCH maximum is not a recognized trigger or biomarker for AKI. Early postoperative extremes are more reflective of lab variability or transfusion effects than renal injury.
MCH maxima do not meaningfully reflect renal hypoperfusion, congestion, or nephrotoxic exposure. Any signal would be weak and non-specific.
Late-window MCH maximum remains clinically non-informative for AKI prediction. Prefer avoiding redundancy with other anemia/oxygen delivery measures if present.
Low MCH is typically a chronic microcytic pattern and not an acute postoperative renal risk marker. In 0–12h it is unlikely to represent a kidney-relevant insult.
MCH minimum has no established direct relationship with postoperative creatinine rise. Any indirect link via anemia is better captured by hemoglobin/hematocrit and hemodynamics.
Even as a leading indicator window, MCH minimum is not a meaningful physiologic predictor of AKI. It is largely unrelated to renal perfusion or tubular injury.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects ordering patterns (CBC frequency) rather than renal pathophysiology. In the immediate post-op period CBCs are routine, so it is a weak acuity proxy.
CBC/MCH measurement count is largely standard ICU monitoring and not specific to impending AKI. At most it weakly reflects overall instability/bleeding surveillance.
Later CBC frequency may reflect ongoing issues (bleeding/transfusion), but it is still a behavioral proxy and non-specific for AKI. Score kept low per measured-feature rule.

Dysnatremia can reflect fluid balance, neurohormonal stress, and severity of illness, which correlate indirectly with AKI risk. In 0–12h, sodium is often influenced by bypass/fluids and is less specific, so importance is limited.

Persistent sodium abnormalities may indicate ongoing free-water/volume derangements and illness severity that can accompany AKI. However it remains an indirect marker rather than a direct renal injury signal.

By 25–36h, sustained dysnatremia more likely reflects true ongoing volume/free-water imbalance and physiologic stress, which can track with renal hypoperfusion or congestion. Still indirect, but closer to outcome and more informative than earlier epochs.

Hypernatremia extremes can indicate significant free-water deficit or hypertonic exposure and are associated with worse outcomes, including AKI, but causality is indirect. In the immediate 0–12h post-op period, sodium spikes are often iatrogenic/transient, so score is conservative.

Hypernatremia can reflect free-water deficit/diuresis and overall illness severity, which may accompany AKI, but it is not a direct renal injury marker. In the 13–24h trajectory phase it is only a modest indirect signal.

Later hypernatremia may indicate ongoing dehydration, osmotic diuresis, or aggressive diuresis, which can worsen renal perfusion. Still, sodium is nonspecific for intrinsic AKI, so it remains an indirect predictor.

Early postoperative hyponatremia is common from hemodilution, fluids, and CPB-related shifts and is rarely a causal driver of AKI. Given the 0–12h window’s transient derangements, prognostic value is weak.
Persistent hyponatremia can reflect fluid overload/heart failure physiology and neurohormonal activation, which correlate with renal congestion and AKI risk. It is still nonspecific and indirect.
Later hyponatremia may signal ongoing volume overload and low effective arterial volume, both associated with cardiorenal syndrome and AKI. It is not a direct biomarker of renal injury.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects routine ICU lab cadence immediately post-op rather than kidney pathophysiology. In the first 12 hours it is especially protocol-driven.
Sodium checks are commonly bundled with BMPs and reflect monitoring intensity more than impending AKI. Without a specific renal-focused trigger, this is a weak behavioral proxy.

More frequent sodium monitoring late may reflect clinician concern about fluid balance/diuresis or evolving organ dysfunction, which can co-occur with AKI. Still, it is a nonspecific behavioral marker and should stay low.

Ordering TEG platelet aggregation is primarily a bleeding/coagulopathy management decision and has no direct mechanistic link to AKI. In the immediate post-op period it is often routine for hemostasis assessment.
This test reflects ongoing evaluation of platelet function/antiplatelet effect rather than renal perfusion or tubular injury. Any association with AKI would be confounded by overall acuity.
Late TEG platelet aggregation testing still primarily indicates bleeding/thrombosis management and transfusion planning, not kidney injury. It is at best an indirect severity marker.
MCHC is not a recognized predictor of postoperative AKI and is largely influenced by anemia/hemodilution and lab variability. Early post-CPB changes are common and not kidney-specific.
Average MCHC over 13–24h has minimal physiologic connection to renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. It is unlikely to add independent predictive value for creatinine rise.
Even close to the outcome window, MCHC is not a direct marker of renal injury or perfusion status. Any signal would be weak and likely collinear with broader anemia/volume status measures.
An extreme (max) MCHC is more likely to reflect hemoconcentration or analytic artifact than a causal AKI pathway. In the immediate post-op window it is not a reliable renal risk indicator.
A higher MCHC maximum may loosely correlate with hemoconcentration, but it is not an established AKI predictor and is prone to noise. Given limited mechanistic specificity, importance remains low.
MCHC extremes mainly reflect hemoconcentration/transfusion effects rather than renal injury; at 25–36h it may weakly proxy volume status but is not a primary AKI driver.
Early postoperative low MCHC is usually dilutional or related to perioperative blood loss/transfusion and is not a specific predictor of AKI; 0–12h values are often transient.
Low MCHC in this window remains a nonspecific anemia/dilution marker with limited direct linkage to renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury.
Persistently low MCHC may reflect ongoing hemodilution/bleeding and higher illness severity, which can indirectly relate to AKI risk, but it is not a direct renal marker.
Measurement frequency reflects ordering behavior and routine CBC monitoring after cardiac surgery rather than a physiologic mechanism for AKI.
CBC/MCHC rechecks are common ICU practice; ordering frequency is a weak, nonspecific proxy for acuity and not a direct AKI predictor.
More frequent checks late may indicate ongoing bleeding/transfusion or instability, which can correlate with AKI risk, but it remains an indirect behavioral proxy.
Mean hematocrit early is heavily influenced by bypass-related hemodilution and transfusion; anemia can reduce oxygen delivery but 0–12h averages are often transient and nonspecific for AKI.
Sustained anemia can contribute to renal hypoxia, but the mean is less informative than the nadir and this mid window is still relatively early for predicting hour-60 creatinine rise.
Persistently low hematocrit closer to the outcome can reflect ongoing bleeding/hemodilution and reduced oxygen delivery, which are established contributors to AKI risk, though not definitive alone.
High hematocrit early often reflects perioperative hemoconcentration or transfusion and is not a typical trigger for AKI; early extremes are frequently transient.
A higher hematocrit in this phase is generally not a recognized AKI driver and is less pathologically informative than low hematocrit.
Late hemoconcentration can weakly suggest hypovolemia/over-diuresis and reduced renal perfusion, but it is an indirect and nonspecific signal.
The hematocrit nadir captures acute anemia/hemodilution and potential reduced oxygen delivery, which is a known AKI risk factor, but early postoperative nadirs are common and may be transient.
A low nadir in this trajectory window suggests persistent anemia/bleeding and impaired oxygen delivery, an established contributor to postoperative AKI risk.

A low hematocrit nadir close to hour 60 strongly signals ongoing anemia/bleeding/hemodilution with reduced renal oxygen delivery and higher illness severity, making it a strong predictor of subsequent creatinine rise.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects bleeding/anemia surveillance and clinician concern rather than a direct AKI mechanism. Early (0–12h) post-CPB labs are routine, so prognostic specificity for creatinine rise is limited.

More frequent hematocrit checks can proxy ongoing bleeding/transfusion needs and hemodynamic instability, which can secondarily affect renal perfusion. Still a behavioral/acuity marker rather than a direct renal predictor.

Late increased hematocrit monitoring may indicate persistent instability or anemia management that could correlate with AKI risk, but it remains an indirect clinician-behavior signal. Per rules, measured features should stay low unless highly specific for impending AKI.
Basophil count has no established physiologic link to postoperative AKI or renal hypoperfusion. Early postoperative leukocyte differential shifts are nonspecific.
Mean basophil count is not a recognized predictor of AKI and is unlikely to add signal beyond general inflammation markers. Any association would be incidental/collinear with broader CBC changes.
Transient extremes in basophils are not known to reflect renal injury or perfusion deficits. In the immediate post-op window, such extremes are typically noise or lab variability.
A max basophil value does not capture a plausible AKI trigger or biomarker. It is unlikely to be clinically actionable for renal risk stratification.
Low basophil counts are not mechanistically tied to AKI after cardiac surgery. This is not a meaningful renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity indicator.
Minimum basophil count remains clinically nonspecific and not linked to kidney injury pathways. Any correlation would likely be spurious or collinear with overall leukocyte trends.

Basophils are typically reported as part of routine CBC differentials; measurement frequency reflects routine ordering rather than AKI suspicion. It provides minimal incremental information for predicting creatinine rise.
CBC differential ordering frequency is a weak proxy for general acuity/infection workup, not specifically renal risk. Thus it has little direct relevance to creatinine_ratio.

Even in the closest window, basophil measurement frequency is not a targeted AKI surveillance behavior (unlike creatinine/urine output). It is therefore unlikely to predict creatinine_ratio meaningfully.

Percent lymphocytes mainly reflects postoperative stress response and hemodilution rather than renal perfusion or tubular injury. In 0–12h, these shifts are common and transient, limiting AKI specificity.
Persistently low lymphocyte percentage can correlate with systemic inflammation/sepsis risk, which can secondarily contribute to AKI. As a mean differential metric, it is indirect and nonspecific.

An early maximum lymphocyte percentage is not a known AKI trigger or biomarker and is heavily influenced by perioperative leukocyte shifts. Extremes here are unlikely to reflect renal pathophysiology.

A higher max lymphocyte percentage may weakly reflect recovery from stress leukogram or evolving inflammatory states, but links to AKI are indirect. It may add limited signal as a nonspecific illness-severity marker.

Low lymphocyte percentage is a nonspecific stress/inflammation marker and not a direct driver of postoperative AKI. In the immediate 0–12h post-CPB period, leukocyte shifts are common and transient, limiting prognostic value.

Persistently low lymphocyte percentage may reflect ongoing stress or infection risk but has weak, indirect linkage to renal perfusion or tubular injury. Not an established predictor of creatinine rise.
Measurement frequency reflects routine CBC ordering rather than renal pathophysiology. In early postoperative care this is standard and not specific for impending AKI.
CBC monitoring frequency is largely protocol-driven and only weakly reflects acuity. It does not directly indicate renal injury risk.
Even closer to outcome, repeated lymphocyte percent checks are not a typical AKI surveillance behavior compared with creatinine/urine output. Thus it remains a weak, nonspecific proxy.

Ordering a vancomycin level suggests exposure to a potentially nephrotoxic antibiotic and clinician concern for dosing/renal clearance. In 0–12h this is often early stewardship and less specific for evolving AKI.
Vancomycin level monitoring indicates ongoing vancomycin therapy and attention to renal clearance, which is plausibly linked to AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy rather than a physiologic marker.

Closer to hour-60 creatinine, checking random vancomycin levels may reflect concern for accumulation from declining GFR and nephrotoxicity risk. However it remains indirect and should not be scored high.

Trough monitoring implies vancomycin exposure and potential nephrotoxic risk, but in the immediate postoperative window it may be routine timing rather than AKI-specific concern. It is not a direct biomarker of renal injury.
Trough measurement suggests ongoing dosing where renal function affects drug clearance, indirectly relating to AKI risk. As a measurement-frequency feature, it should remain low importance.
Late trough monitoring can reflect concern for impaired clearance and higher nephrotoxic exposure, which can precede creatinine rise. Still indirect and confounded by indication and dosing protocols.

Lower sustained oxygenation can contribute to systemic hypoxia and renal ischemic susceptibility, but early post-op hypoxemia is common and often transient. Mean SpO2 also smooths extremes that better capture acute insults.

Ongoing hypoxemia during the trajectory phase may indicate respiratory/cardiac dysfunction that can impair renal oxygen delivery. Mean values remain an indirect marker and less informative than extremes.

Persistently reduced oxygen saturation closer to the outcome can reflect ongoing cardiopulmonary failure and reduced renal oxygen delivery, increasing AKI risk. As a mean, it captures sustained hypoxemia rather than brief dips, making it moderately informative.

Maximum SpO2 largely reflects supplemental oxygen/ventilator settings and is typically near 100%, offering little discrimination for AKI risk. It does not capture hypoxic insult (the relevant pathology would be low minima).

As with 0–12h, SpO2 maximum is usually ceiling-limited and driven by oxygen therapy rather than physiology relevant to renal injury. It adds redundancy without capturing the harmful extreme (hypoxemia).

High/maximum oxygen saturation is not a pathologic insult and is unlikely to directly relate to renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. It is also collinear with other oxygenation summaries, with minima being more clinically meaningful.

Early postoperative hypoxemia can contribute to systemic stress and renal ischemia, but in the immediate 0–12h period transient desaturations are common after cardiac surgery and less specific for later AKI.

Persistent hypoxemia into 13–24h may reflect evolving pulmonary dysfunction and higher illness severity that can secondarily worsen renal perfusion. Still an indirect marker rather than a primary AKI driver.

Hypoxemia closest to the outcome window can indicate ongoing cardiopulmonary failure and impaired oxygen delivery, which is associated with renal ischemic injury risk. The minimum captures acute insults better than mean/max.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a physiologic mechanism for AKI. In the immediate post-op period, frequent SpO2 documentation is routine.
SpO2 measurement frequency is largely a care-process variable and is not a direct predictor of renal injury. Differences are more likely due to workflow/monitoring practices than AKI pathophysiology.
More frequent SpO2 checks late may reflect clinician concern for instability (e.g., respiratory failure/shock), which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, not a direct biomarker.

ALT reflects hepatocellular injury rather than kidney function; early postoperative elevations can be transient from surgery/CPB. Mean values are also less informative than extremes for acute hypoperfusion signals.

A high early ALT peak can suggest ischemic hepatitis from low-flow states, which often co-occur with renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk. However, in 0–12h it is still relatively nonspecific and may be transient.
Low/minimum ALT has no meaningful physiologic link to AKI and does not capture a relevant insult. It is unlikely to add signal beyond other markers of shock.
ALT ordering frequency is a care-process marker and is not directly tied to renal injury mechanisms. In early post-op care, liver enzymes may be checked routinely or for non-renal reasons.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician behavior and is not a direct AKI predictor. ALT checks may relate to hepatic concerns rather than impending renal dysfunction.
Late repeated ALT testing may indicate concern for ongoing low-flow/ischemic injury, which can correlate with AKI risk. Nonetheless it remains an indirect, non-physiologic proxy variable.

BUN is a kidney-related lab influenced by renal perfusion, catabolism, and volume status; early elevations can indicate prerenal azotemia. In 0–12h, however, it may be confounded by perioperative fluids and hemodilution.
BUN trends in 13–24h can reflect evolving prerenal physiology or early renal dysfunction and correlate with AKI risk. Mean is acceptable here, but it is less specific than creatinine/urine output.

Elevated BUN closer to hour 60 can indicate sustained prerenal azotemia or developing intrinsic renal dysfunction, making it a strong predictor of subsequent creatinine rise. This window is temporally proximal and more likely to reflect true trajectory rather than transient post-op effects.

BUN can reflect pre-existing renal dysfunction, hypoperfusion, or catabolism, but in the immediate post-op window it is noisy (hemodilution, transfusion, CPB effects). Max is more informative than mean/min for acute azotemia, but early timing limits specificity for later creatinine rise.

Persistently elevated or rising BUN by 13–24h suggests ongoing renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI and is more prognostic than the immediate post-op period. Max captures episodic azotemia better than mean/min.

High BUN close to the outcome window is a strong indicator of evolving renal dysfunction and correlates with subsequent creatinine rise. The maximum value best captures clinically meaningful azotemia and reduces redundancy versus mean/min.
Low BUN early after surgery is usually driven by hemodilution/fluids and does not indicate AKI risk. The minimum is not a pathophysiologic trigger for creatinine rise.
A low minimum BUN in this window is generally non-informative for AKI and often reflects dilution or nutrition status rather than renal injury. It adds little beyond other BUN summaries.

Minimum BUN near 25–36h is not a meaningful marker of renal injury and is typically influenced by fluid balance rather than kidney function decline. Extremes relevant to AKI are better captured by BUN_max.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects routine post-cardiac surgery lab protocols rather than specific AKI physiology in the first 12 hours. As a behavioral proxy, it has limited incremental prognostic value.

More frequent BUN checks may reflect clinician concern for renal dysfunction or hemodynamic instability, but it is still an indirect behavioral signal. It should be down-weighted versus actual BUN values.

Increased BUN monitoring late (25–36h) can indicate evolving AKI or worsening perfusion prompting closer surveillance, but it remains non-physiologic. Cap importance to avoid overinterpreting ordering behavior.

Amylase testing relates to pancreatitis/ischemia workup and is not a standard or direct predictor of post-cardiac surgery AKI. Ordering in the immediate post-op period is unlikely to add renal-specific signal.
Amylase measurement is an indirect marker of non-renal complications and does not mechanistically predict creatinine rise. As an ordering proxy, it has weak relevance to AKI.
Even close to outcome, amylase measurement primarily reflects evaluation for abdominal pathology rather than kidney injury. It is not a recognized AKI biomarker and should be low importance.

Acidemia can worsen renal perfusion and reflects shock/lactate burden, but early post-CPB acid-base disturbances are common and transient. Mean pH smooths extremes and is less informative than a nadir would be.

Sustained lower pH in this trajectory phase can indicate ongoing hypoperfusion/respiratory failure associated with AKI risk, but mean values may dilute brief severe acidemia. Moderate importance given indirect linkage.

Persistently low mean pH close to hour 60 suggests ongoing shock/poor perfusion or severe respiratory failure, which are established contributors to AKI. Still indirect compared with direct renal labs, and mean is less sensitive than extremes.

Alkalemia (high pH) is not a typical driver of AKI and often reflects ventilation strategy or bicarbonate administration early post-op. The maximum pH is less pathologically relevant than low pH for renal risk.
Alkalemia peaks are rarely a direct driver of AKI and are often ventilator/iatrogenic. To avoid collinearity with pH minima (more pathologic), cap importance.
High pH is not a typical causal pathway for postoperative AKI compared with acidemia/hypoperfusion. Kept low to prioritize pH minima as the clinically meaningful extreme.
Early postoperative acidemia can reflect transient CPB/anesthesia effects and lactate load; it is an indirect marker of hypoperfusion risk. Conservative scoring due to the immediate post-op epoch.
Persistent/worsening acidemia in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing shock, poor perfusion, or respiratory failure, which increases AKI risk. Still not kidney-specific, so not maximal.

Late acidemia close to the outcome window is a strong marker of ongoing hypoperfusion/sepsis/low cardiac output, all strongly linked to ischemic tubular injury. The minimum captures the acute insult better than mean/max.
Frequent blood gas checks reflect clinician concern/instability (ventilation, shock), which correlates with AKI risk but is not mechanistic. Measurement-count features are limited by rule.
More frequent pH/ABG monitoring suggests ongoing instability and higher acuity, indirectly associated with AKI. It remains a behavioral proxy rather than a physiologic driver.

High measurement frequency late suggests persistent critical illness and closer monitoring, which can correlate with impending organ dysfunction including AKI. Scored low per measurement-frequency rule.

Ordering cholesterol in the immediate post-op ICU period is not physiologically linked to AKI and is usually unrelated to acute renal perfusion. As a measurement proxy, it provides minimal prognostic value.
Cholesterol testing is not a typical response to evolving AKI risk and does not reflect renal physiology. Likely noise/administrative rather than acuity-driven.
Late cholesterol measurement is not an established marker of renal injury or hemodynamic compromise. Unlikely to add signal for creatinine rise prediction.
Mean lymphocyte count early post-op is a nonspecific inflammation/stress marker and not a direct AKI predictor. Mean aggregation further dilutes any acute signal.
Sustained lymphopenia can reflect systemic stress/inflammation and has weak associations with adverse outcomes, including AKI, but is indirect. Mean value is less informative than extremes.
A high lymphocyte maximum is not a recognized trigger or marker for AKI in this setting. Prioritize minima (lymphopenia) if selecting among collinear aggregates.
Lymphocyte maxima are generally not clinically meaningful for AKI risk compared with lymphopenia or neutrophil-driven inflammation. Kept low to reduce redundancy.

Early lymphopenia may indicate surgical stress response or systemic inflammation, which can correlate with later organ dysfunction including AKI. Conservative due to early epoch and indirect mechanism.

Lymphopenia can reflect systemic inflammation/stress after surgery, which correlates weakly with organ dysfunction risk, but it is not a direct AKI driver. In the 13–24h trajectory phase it is a nonspecific severity marker.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects ordering behavior and routine post-op labs rather than renal physiology. Early (0–12h) ordering is common and not specific for impending AKI.
This is a behavioral proxy for acuity/lab monitoring and is not a mechanistic predictor of AKI. In 13–24h it remains nonspecific and likely routine.

More frequent CBC monitoring closer to 60h may indicate clinician concern for complications (infection/bleeding) that can secondarily worsen renal perfusion. Still indirect and non-mechanistic, so capped low.

Glucose checks are routine in post-cardiac surgery ICU care (stress hyperglycemia/insulin protocols) and measurement presence is not specific for AKI. As a measured/behavioral feature it should be low.
Ongoing glucose monitoring is standard ICU practice and does not directly indicate renal injury risk. As a measurement-frequency proxy it provides minimal AKI-specific signal.
Even near the outcome window, glucose measurement is common and reflects protocolized care more than AKI pathophysiology. As a measured feature it remains weakly informative.
CK ordering frequency is a behavioral marker and in the immediate 0–12h period may be prompted by surgery-related muscle injury concerns rather than renal injury. Not specific for AKI risk.
CK being checked in 13–24h may reflect concern for rhabdomyolysis/ischemia, which can contribute to AKI, but measurement frequency is still indirect. Therefore kept low.

CK checks closer to 60h can signal clinician concern for ongoing muscle injury/ischemia that could precipitate pigment nephropathy. Still an ordering proxy rather than a physiologic value, so capped at 2.

Early postoperative eGFR (creatinine-derived) reflects baseline renal reserve and immediate post-op renal function, which relates to AKI risk. However 0–12h values can be transiently distorted by hemodilution and fluid shifts, so conservative scoring.
Sustained reduction in eGFR during 13–24h suggests evolving renal dysfunction and is an established predictor of later creatinine rise. Mean is informative but may smooth important lows.

In the 25–36h leading window, lower mean eGFR strongly indicates ongoing renal impairment and is closely tied to subsequent creatinine at 60h. Still, mean may dilute acute nadirs compared with a minimum.

A higher (max) eGFR is generally not pathologic and is often driven by hemodilution/low creatinine early after bypass. To avoid collinearity with eGFR_mean and because extremes of high eGFR are not AKI triggers, score is low.
Max eGFR mainly captures the best (least impaired) moment and is not a marker of renal injury; it is also redundant with mean eGFR. It provides little AKI-specific prognostic value.

Even close to the outcome, the maximum eGFR reflects the least impaired period and is not a trigger/biomarker of AKI. It is collinear with eGFR_mean and less pathophysiologically relevant than low eGFR measures.
eGFR is directly derived from creatinine and reflects early renal dysfunction, but in the immediate 0–12h post-op period values can be transiently distorted by hemodilution and peri-CPB shifts.
A low nadir eGFR in the trajectory phase suggests evolving renal impairment beyond immediate post-op noise, but still may reflect ongoing fluid shifts and delayed equilibration.
Low eGFR closest to the outcome window is essentially an early biomarker of rising creatinine/AKI and strongly predicts the creatinine ratio at 60h.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects ordering behavior and routine post-cardiac surgery lab protocols rather than a physiologic driver of AKI in this early window.
More frequent eGFR/chemistry checks can indicate clinician concern for renal dysfunction, but it is an indirect acuity proxy rather than a mechanism.
Increased monitoring late (25–36h) may reflect suspected AKI and escalation of care, but remains a behavioral signal and should be kept low to avoid confounding.
LDH ordering frequency is largely protocol- or workup-driven (hemolysis/ischemia) and is not a specific physiologic predictor of AKI.
LDH measurement count is an indirect marker of illness evaluation and has weak, nonspecific linkage to renal injury risk.
Even late, LDH measurement frequency is a nonspecific clinician-behavior proxy and does not directly track kidney perfusion or tubular injury.
Coagulopathy can correlate with hepatic dysfunction, bleeding/transfusion, and overall severity that may contribute to AKI, but early post-op INR is often transient and treatment-related.
Persistently elevated average INR later may indicate ongoing critical illness (shock liver, bleeding, anticoagulation complexity) associated with AKI risk, though it is not kidney-specific.
An early peak INR may reflect CPB-related coagulopathy or anticoagulation effects; it is a weak-to-moderate severity marker but not a direct AKI trigger.
A late high INR peak can signal significant ongoing derangement (hepatic hypoperfusion, bleeding/transfusion burden) that is associated with renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk.
Low INR is generally not a plausible driver of AKI and is less informative than high extremes for severity; keep low to reduce redundancy with max/mean.
The minimum INR provides little pathophysiologic signal for AKI compared with elevated INR measures and is likely redundant.
INR measurement frequency is mostly protocolized after cardiac surgery and reflects monitoring behavior rather than a direct predictor of renal injury.

INR ordering frequency is mainly a proxy for bleeding risk/anticoagulation management and overall acuity rather than a direct AKI mechanism. In the 13–24h window it may weakly reflect instability/transfusion needs that can affect renal perfusion.

More frequent INR checks late (25–36h) can indicate ongoing bleeding, liver dysfunction, or anticoagulation titration, which may correlate with shock/transfusion exposure. Still a behavioral marker, not a direct renal biomarker.
Eosinophil percentage is not a typical driver or early marker of post-cardiac surgery AKI. Early postoperative values are often nonspecific and influenced by stress response and steroids.

Mean eosinophil percentage in this epoch has little established relationship to hemodynamic AKI after cardiac surgery. It is more relevant to rare allergic/drug-induced interstitial nephritis, which is uncommon acutely here.
Transient eosinophil spikes early after surgery are usually nonspecific and not a recognized AKI trigger in this setting. Extremes here do not reliably indicate renal injury.
Even if elevated, eosinophilia is an uncommon and delayed signal for AIN rather than typical post-CPB AKI. The max adds little clinically meaningful AKI prediction in this timeframe.
Low eosinophils are common with physiologic stress and steroid exposure and do not predict renal hypoperfusion or tubular injury. The minimum is not pathognomonic for AKI.
A low eosinophil percentage in this window reflects stress leukogram effects rather than kidney-specific pathology. Not an established AKI predictor.
Measurement frequency largely reflects routine CBC ordering in the immediate post-op period rather than targeted AKI concern. As a behavioral proxy, it provides minimal incremental AKI signal.
CBC differential measurement count is mostly standard ICU practice and not specific to impending AKI. At most it reflects general acuity, so importance remains low.
Even late, eosinophil measurement frequency is not typically driven by AKI suspicion in post-cardiac surgery patients. It is more routine monitoring than a renal-specific signal.

LDL testing is unrelated to acute renal perfusion/tubular injury and is not part of standard AKI evaluation. Ordering it early is usually incidental (baseline risk profiling) rather than AKI-relevant.

LDL measurement in the ICU is not clinically tied to AKI development and is unlikely to reflect renal risk or hemodynamic compromise. Primarily administrative/long-term cardiovascular risk assessment.
Late LDL ordering remains unrelated to AKI mechanisms and does not serve as a proxy for renal deterioration. Provides negligible predictive value for creatinine rise.

MCV reflects chronic anemia etiologies (e.g., B12/folate deficiency, liver disease) and is not a direct AKI driver. In the immediate post-op period it is unlikely to change meaningfully or predict creatinine trajectory.
MCV in this epoch remains a nonspecific hematologic index with weak linkage to post-cardiac surgery AKI. Any association would be indirect via comorbidity burden, so importance is low.

MCV is not a direct marker of renal perfusion or tubular injury; postoperative shifts usually reflect anemia/transfusion or dilution rather than AKI. Mean values are also nonspecific for acute insults.

Early postoperative MCV extremes are typically driven by transfusion, hemodilution, or lab variability and have no established causal link to AKI. The 0–12h window is especially confounded by CPB-related transient changes.
MCV maxima in this period remain nonspecific and are not an established predictor of postoperative AKI. Any association would be indirect via bleeding/transfusion rather than kidney injury itself.

Even close to the outcome window, elevated MCV is not a recognized leading indicator of AKI after cardiac surgery. It mainly reflects erythrocyte indices/transfusion effects rather than renal pathophysiology.

Low MCV in the immediate postoperative period reflects baseline microcytosis or dilution and does not directly inform renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. Early epoch changes are common and not specific for AKI.
MCV minima are not mechanistically linked to AKI and are unlikely to add prognostic value beyond anemia/bleeding variables. This is not a standard AKI risk marker.
Low MCV near 36h remains a weak, indirect proxy at best (e.g., chronic anemia) and is not an established predictor of creatinine rise. Not a renal biomarker.
Measurement frequency reflects routine CBC ordering after cardiac surgery rather than specific concern for AKI. As a behavioral proxy, it provides minimal renal-specific signal.
CBC/MCV rechecks are common ICU practice and usually track anemia/bleeding, not impending AKI. As a _measured feature, it is a weak acuity proxy.
More frequent MCV measurements typically indicate ongoing hematologic monitoring (bleeding/transfusion) rather than renal injury surveillance. Behavioral signal is weak for AKI prediction.

Mean platelet volume is not a direct marker of renal perfusion or tubular injury; postoperative changes are nonspecific and influenced by inflammation/consumption. Mean aggregation further dilutes any acute signal.
MPV mean has limited evidence as an AKI predictor in this setting and is largely a nonspecific inflammatory/platelet activation marker. Trajectory-phase mean values are unlikely to be informative.

Persistently abnormal MPV may weakly reflect systemic inflammation or platelet activation that can accompany severe illness and AKI risk, but it is indirect and not kidney-specific. Mean values are less informative than extremes.
Early MPV maxima are nonspecific and heavily confounded by CPB-related platelet activation and perioperative inflammation. Not an established direct predictor of postoperative creatinine rise.

Higher MPV extremes may indicate heightened inflammatory/platelet activation states associated with critical illness, which can correlate with AKI risk, but the link is indirect. Max is preferred over mean for capturing acute derangement.

In the 25–36h leading-indicator window, elevated MPV maxima could reflect ongoing systemic inflammation/consumption that tracks with severity and AKI risk, but it remains nonspecific and not a renal biomarker. Max captures acute derangement better than mean, limiting redundancy.

Mean platelet volume is not a direct marker of renal perfusion or tubular injury; low MPV is at most a nonspecific inflammatory/hematologic signal. In the immediate 0–12h post-op window it is unlikely to add specific AKI prognostic value.
MPV extremes are not established predictors of post-cardiac surgery AKI and are largely nonspecific. Any association would be indirect via inflammation/consumption rather than kidney-specific injury.

Persistently low MPV late may reflect ongoing systemic inflammation/consumption or critical illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. It remains nonspecific and not a primary renal-pathophysiology driver.
Measurement frequency reflects ordering behavior and routine CBC monitoring after cardiac surgery rather than a kidney-specific mechanism. It is unlikely to uniquely indicate impending AKI in 0–12h.
CBC/MPV measurement counts mainly capture routine ICU surveillance and general acuity, not renal injury. As a behavioral proxy it should be down-weighted.
Even late, MPV measurement frequency is more reflective of ongoing monitoring intensity than a causal pathway to AKI. It provides limited incremental signal beyond other severity markers.

Elevated lactate reflects global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output that can contribute to renal ischemia, but early post-CPB lactate elevations are common and often transient. The mean further smooths peaks, reducing specificity for AKI triggers.

Sustained lactate elevation in the trajectory phase suggests persistent shock/poor perfusion, a recognized contributor to ischemic AKI after cardiac surgery. Mean lactate captures ongoing hypoperfusion burden better here than in 0–12h.

A high lactate peak indicates an acute hypoperfusion insult (e.g., low cardiac output, bleeding, vasoplegia) that can precipitate renal ischemia. However, immediate post-op peaks can be influenced by CPB and are less specific early.

A high lactate maximum at 13–24h strongly suggests ongoing or recurrent hypoperfusion/shock, which is directly linked to subsequent AKI risk. The extreme value better captures the injurious episode than the mean.
The minimum lactate mainly reflects periods of adequate perfusion and is not a trigger for AKI. It is less informative than lactate maxima for identifying hypoperfusion-related renal risk.
Low/normal lactate minima do not indicate renal injury risk and are generally reassuring rather than predictive. This aggregation is also redundant given max/mean lactate.

More frequent lactate checks can indicate clinician concern for shock/instability, which correlates with AKI risk, but it is an indirect behavioral proxy. Early post-op lactate monitoring is also common, limiting specificity.

Increased lactate measurement frequency at 13–24h often reflects ongoing hemodynamic concern and resuscitation, indirectly associated with AKI. Still, it is not a physiologic biomarker and should be kept low.

Late frequent lactate monitoring suggests persistent or worsening instability close to the outcome window, which can correlate with AKI risk. As an ordering-intensity proxy, it remains indirect and capped at 2.

Alkaline phosphatase is primarily a hepatobiliary/bone marker and is not a direct indicator of renal perfusion or tubular injury. Early post-op changes are nonspecific and unlikely to predict creatinine rise at 60h.
Alkaline phosphatase reflects cholestasis/bone turnover rather than renal perfusion or tubular injury. Early post-op fluctuations are common and not a specific AKI signal.
Low ALP has no established mechanistic link to postoperative AKI risk. It is unlikely to add prognostic value for creatinine rise.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects ordering behavior and routine post-op lab panels rather than kidney injury physiology. In the immediate 0–12h period it is especially nonspecific.
Ordering ALP in this window typically reflects liver/biliary evaluation or routine labs, not targeted AKI surveillance. As a behavioral proxy it has weak specificity for impending creatinine rise.
Even close to the outcome, ALP ordering is not a typical trigger for AKI concern compared with creatinine/urine output/electrolytes. As a measured-count feature it remains a weak, indirect proxy.

RDW is a nonspecific marker of chronic illness/inflammation and does not directly reflect renal perfusion or tubular injury. In the immediate post-op period, mean values add little signal and are collinear with other RDW summaries.

Persistently elevated RDW can correlate with illness severity and inflammatory stress, which may associate with AKI risk indirectly. Mean aggregation is less informative than extremes and may be redundant.

Later RDW may track ongoing systemic stress/hemolysis/transfusion burden that correlates with complications including AKI, but it is not a direct renal marker. Mean values are still a smoothed, indirect signal.

An early high RDW can reflect preexisting frailty/anemia/inflammation and perioperative transfusion/hemolysis, which are associated with worse outcomes including AKI. However, in 0–12h it is conservative and nonspecific.
A higher RDW peak in this trajectory phase may indicate ongoing physiologic stress or transfusion needs, indirectly increasing AKI risk. It remains a weak-to-moderate, non-causal predictor.

A late RDW peak may better capture ongoing inflammation/hemolysis/transfusion burden and overall severity, which are established correlates of postoperative AKI. Among RDW summaries, the extreme is more likely to capture clinically meaningful derangement than the mean/min.
Low/normal RDW is not a recognized trigger or marker for AKI. The minimum value is unlikely to capture pathology relevant to creatinine rise.
RDW minimum provides little information about physiologic insult or severity and is not mechanistically linked to AKI. It is also redundant given other RDW aggregations.
Even in the leading-indicator window, RDW minimum is not clinically meaningful for AKI prediction compared with peaks or direct renal/hemodynamic markers. Likely adds noise/collinearity.
RDW measurement count reflects CBC ordering frequency, which is routine after cardiac surgery and not specific to AKI concern. As a behavioral proxy in 0–12h it has minimal prognostic value.

CBC/RDW ordering frequency in this window mainly reflects general acuity or routine monitoring rather than targeted renal risk assessment. As a measured-count feature it should be kept low importance.
Measurement frequency reflects ordering behavior rather than renal physiology; RDW itself is not an AKI biomarker. Even in the 25–36h window, this is more a nonspecific acuity/anemia workup signal.

Early post-op chloride averages are heavily influenced by intraop/ICU fluid choices and transient shifts, with weak direct linkage to later creatinine rise. Mean is also less informative than extremes for detecting hyperchloremic insult.

Sustained hyperchloremia can contribute to renal vasoconstriction and metabolic acidosis, but the mean still smooths clinically relevant peaks. Mid-epoch values are less proximal to the 60h creatinine outcome.

Later sustained chloride elevation may reflect ongoing high-chloride resuscitation and acid-base disturbance, plausibly affecting renal perfusion. However, mean is collinear with max/min and is less specific than extremes.

Peak hyperchloremia can indicate large-volume normal saline exposure and hyperchloremic acidosis, which may reduce renal blood flow. In the immediate 0–12h period this is often transient and thus only a modest predictor.
A high chloride peak in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing chloride load and acid-base stress beyond the OR period. Still an indirect contributor rather than a primary AKI driver.

Late peak hyperchloremia is a more proximal signal of continued high-chloride resuscitation/acid-base derangement and may correlate with worse renal perfusion and AKI risk. Among chloride summaries, the max best captures the potentially injurious extreme.

Low chloride is not a typical direct trigger for post-cardiac surgery AKI and often reflects dilution or diuretic/alkalosis effects. In the immediate post-op window it is commonly transient and nonspecific.

Chloride minima are generally less mechanistically linked to AKI than hyperchloremia and are often confounded by diuretics and fluid shifts. Provides limited incremental prognostic value versus chloride max.
Even close to the outcome, low chloride is not an established AKI predictor and is typically a nonspecific marker of alkalosis/diuretic effect. Likely redundant and weak compared with chloride max.
Test frequency is primarily a proxy for routine post-op monitoring rather than kidney injury physiology. In 0–12h, chloride is commonly checked by protocol, limiting its acuity signal.
Ordering frequency may reflect general illness severity but is not specific for impending AKI. Chloride checks remain common in ICU care, so this is a weak behavioral proxy.
More frequent electrolyte checks late may indicate clinician concern for instability/acid-base issues, which can co-occur with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy and should be kept low by rule.

HDL measurement is not part of AKI physiology and is rarely ordered for renal risk stratification in the immediate post-op ICU period. This mainly reflects non-renal workflow or baseline lipid evaluation.
HDL ordering is not a meaningful indicator of renal perfusion or tubular injury and is unlikely to be driven by AKI concern. Provides minimal prognostic information.
Even in the leading-indicator window, HDL measurement frequency is not plausibly tied to AKI suspicion or mechanism. It is best treated as non-informative noise for creatinine_ratio prediction.

Post-op neutrophil predominance mainly reflects surgical stress/inflammation and is not a direct driver of renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. Mean in the immediate 0–12h window is especially nonspecific.

Persistently elevated neutrophil percentage may reflect ongoing inflammation/infection that can contribute to AKI risk, but it is indirect and nonspecific. Mean aggregation further dilutes acute inflammatory spikes.

A high early peak neutrophil percentage can signal a strong inflammatory response (e.g., CPB-related), which is associated with AKI risk, but remains indirect. In 0–12h this is common and often transient, so importance is capped.

A later peak may indicate evolving infection/SIRS, which can predispose to AKI via cytokine-mediated injury and hemodynamic instability. Still not a direct renal biomarker and can be collinear with other illness-severity markers.
Low neutrophil percentage is not a typical mechanistic predictor of post-cardiac surgery AKI. The minimum value is more likely noise or differential shifts rather than renal-relevant physiology.
Minimum neutrophil percentage in this period has no established direct relationship to renal injury after cardiac surgery. It is unlikely to add prognostic value beyond overall acuity markers.
Measurement frequency reflects ordering behavior (CBC differentials) and routine post-op monitoring rather than renal pathophysiology. In 0–12h it is near-universal and poorly discriminative.
More frequent checks may reflect clinician concern for infection/instability, but it is a weak, indirect proxy and not a mechanism for AKI. Typically driven by routine ICU protocols.
In the 25–36h leading-indicator window, repeated differentials can reflect escalating concern for sepsis/SIRS, which increases AKI risk. Still an ordering-behavior proxy, so capped at 2.
